schools.summary: Henry reads the verse as a study of motive — the single eye — and lists Solomon's four inducements: the glory of God, the choice of Jerusalem, David's intention, and the performance of God's word.

Clarke takes the cloud as the token that God had accepted the house (cf. Moses at the tabernacle, Exod. 40:34); Wesley reads the 'thick darkness' as God's pavilion, while he dwells in unapproachable light.
Ellicott fixes the 'thick darkness' as araphel, 'gloom of clouds' (Sinai language), and judges the Targum's 'Jerusalem' at 1 Kings 8:12 a gloss.

themes: the God of the thick darkness

schools.summary: Ellicott hears the emphatic 'I, on my part, have built,' and dwells on the poetic diction — zebul ('habitation') a word of only five occurrences, 'a place for thy dwelling' echoing Exod. 15:17, 'for ever' meaning 'through ages.'

themes: the God who consents to a home

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the bronze scaffold is peculiar to Chronicles (a 'pan,' kiyor, like an inverted laver), the kneeling-with-outspread-hands posture is figured on Egyptian monuments, and the chronicler uses the exact form for 'toward heaven.'

themes: a kneeling king in full view
humility at the height of glory

schools.summary: Henry treats the prayer as consecrating the temple a figure of Christ the Mediator, and reads it as a treasury of doctrine (God incomparable, immense, heart-knowing, no sinless perfection) and of ordered petition — 'the prayer that will speed must be warranted by the word.'
Ellicott cautions that 'stood' means only 'took his place' — not that he remained standing, since v.13 shows him kneeling — and that the hands were spread toward heaven.

themes: the temple a figure of Christ

schools.summary: Henry sets God's incomparable perfection at the head of the prayer — the Creator has no peer — and reads the covenant-and-mercy as the reward of those who walk before him with all their hearts.
Ellicott: 'in heaven nor in earth' abridged from Kings; 'keepest covenant and shewest mercy' is 'the covenanted mercy' (Isa. 55:3); the Assyrians too called their gods 'without an equal.'
Clarke deepens 'with all their hearts' from the Targum ('the will of their souls, the affection of their hearts'); Wesley reads the whole prayer as making the temple a figure of Christ the Mediator.
Henry reads the verse as the confession of the divine immensity — the heaven of heavens cannot contain God, nor is anything added to his felicity by our service.
Clarke enlarges the wonder through the Targum's threefold heavens that cannot bear the glory of God's majesty, still less this house.
Ellicott sharpens the incredulous 'what? will God in very deed dwell?' and marks the chronicler's own added 'with men,' pointing forward to Rev. 21:3.
Clarke frames the verse as the first of the seven cases, referring to the fuller treatment at 1 Kings 8:31-46.
Ellicott fixes the legal act — the accused 'enter upon an oath before thine altar' (cf. Exod. 22:11; Ezek. 17:13) — the versions reading 'come and swear.'
Henry: disputants appeal to God by oath; the religious reverence of an oath is ancient and will last as long as any relics of conscience remain.

themes: the first of the seven cases

schools.summary: Clarke fastens on 'there is no man which sinneth not' as a universal truth no descendant of Adam can evade (cf. 1 Kings 8:46).
Ellicott renders 'an enemy' for 'their enemies' and fixes the deportation into captivity, near or far, as the setting of the final plea.
Henry takes the parenthesis as the denial of sinless perfection — 'no man who doeth good and sinneth not' (Eccl. 7:20).
Clarke glosses 'bethink themselves' from the Targum — 'if thy fear should return into their hearts' — the first motion of repentance a revived reverence.

Ellicott renders it 'if they take it to heart,' i.e. repent, and shows the threefold confession a rising climax: 'we have slipped, we have done crookedly, we have been godless' (cf. Ps. 106:6; Dan. 9:5).

Ellicott traces the close to Psalm 132:8-9, marks the chronicler's added 'O LORD God' and the late word for 'resting place,' and objects that the idea of the sanctuary as God's resting-place is 'not in keeping with the spirit of the prayer.'
Wesley reads 'arise into thy resting place' as God coming down to his constant habitation from which he will not remove; Clarke glosses 'rejoice in goodness' of the Levites' tithes and portions.

Henry: Solomon prays God to take and keep possession, making the house his resting-place ('what will the ark do without the God of the ark?'), the priests clothed with salvation to save others, the saints rejoicing in goodness (Ps. 65:4).

debates.question: Does the temple become God's 'resting place,' or is that psalm-borrowed phrase out of keeping with the prayer?

sides.position: The house is God's settled, abiding habitation, into which he comes down to rest among his people and from which he will not remove
'Resting place' is a late word carried over from Psalm 132, not fully in keeping with a prayer that keeps heaven for God's dwelling (cf. v.18)

schools.summary: Ellicott shows the chronicler transposing Psalm 132:10 and forging the phrase 'the mercies of David' (the 'sure mercies' of Isa. 55:3), with the temple-invocation 'O LORD God' as in the priestly blessing (Num. 6:24).
Clarke gives the plea its humble edge from Jarchi — 'if not for my sake, do it for thy own sake' — and notes the two closing verses are the chronicler's own addition, absent from Kings.

Henry hears the two grounds — Solomon's relation to God and the covenant with David — and lifts the 'anointed' through David to Christ, in whom the face of the true Anointed is never turned away (Acts 13:34).

outliers.note: Reads 'thine anointed' and 'the mercies of David' christologically — fulfilled in the Lord Jesus, God's Messiah, whom the Father hears always and never turns away.

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the verse stands word for word as 1 Kings 8:14 — the chronicler copying faithfully — and glosses 'turned' as 'turned round' and 'stood' as 'was standing.'
Ellicott draws out the mouth-and-hands parallel — God 'spake by his mouth and by his hands fulfilled' — and marks the single variant, 'hands' for 'hand,' a difference of vowels only.

themes: praise as the ground of petition
the mouth and the hands of God

schools.summary: Ellicott recovers the clause about choosing no ruler (nagid), likely lost from the older text, and reads it against Saul — 'the people's choice, not God's' — insisting Scripture nowhere makes the voice of the people the voice of God.

outliers.note: Turns the clause 'neither chose I any man to be a ruler' into a caution that vox populi is not vox Dei: Saul was Israel's demand, David God's election.

themes: no city and no ruler until David
the long silence before the double election

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'and I have chosen Jerusalem' and notes some manuscripts omit the verse; the force lies in the twice-spoken divine 'chosen' — city and king together.
Ellicott marks the emphatic 'only thou, thou shalt not build' and the succession 'for thy son that shall come forth,' the rest as in 1 Kings 8:19.
Wesley: 'one sows, another reaps' — David conceived, Solomon executed; no disparagement to build on the good designs of those who went before.
Ellicott renders 'hath performed' as 'hath established,' literally 'caused to rise up' — the spoken promise made to stand in a seated king; 'set' is 'seated,' no real variant from 1 Kings 8:20.
Ellicott reads 'I have set there the ark' as an abridgment of 1 Kings 8:21, and identifies the 'covenant' within the ark as the two tables of the Law (cf. 2 Chron. 5:10).

themes: the covenant as the two tables
a house for the covenant, not an image

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'yet so that' as 'only if' — a single sharp condition — and marks the chronicler's 'walk in my law' for the older 'walk before me,' fixing the dynastic promise upon obedience to the written law.

themes: 'only if' they walk in the law
a sure promise that still asks obedience
the seed of the later history

schools.summary: Ellicott hears 'let thy word be verified' as an echo of David's own prayer at 1 Chron. 17:23, and takes 'thy word' throughout as 'thy promise'; the verse states the principle that faith holds God to what he has spoken.

themes: faith holding God to his word

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'have respect' as 'but turn thou unto,' a verb of God's turning to the suppliant; the older account adds a 'to-day' the chronicler drops.

themes: the infinite God asked to turn

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'upon' as 'toward,' notes 'day and night' for the older 'night and day,' and reads 'toward this place' strictly — the temple as the Kebla toward which prayer is directed (cf. Dan. 6:10).
Henry: Solomon asks God to own the house as the place of his name — 'the prayer that will speed must be warranted by the word' — the abiding centre now being Christ.

themes: God's eyes open day and night
the temple as the Kebla of prayer

schools.summary: Ellicott marks 'supplications' (tahanunim) a rare late word found only here in Chronicles, and 'hear from thy dwelling place, even from heaven' where Kings has the pregnant 'unto.'
Wesley: God helps not without prayer but in answer to it — Christ's intercession does not supersede but encourage our supplications.
Henry: God hears from heaven, his true dwelling, not the temple; and 'when thou hearest, forgive' — pardon is the gate through which every other answer passes.
Ellicott weighs 'requiting the wicked' (older 'find guilty') as the exact antithesis of 'justifying a just one' — condemnation balanced by acquittal.
Henry: God gives judgment according to equity on all appeals, and this we may pray for in faith, 'for we are sure it shall be done' — God on the throne judging right.

themes: condemn the guilty, justify the just
the God who cannot be deceived

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the chronicler's 'be put to the worse' for the older 'are smitten,' and 'when' or 'if they sin' — the same conditional as the drought case at v.26.
Henry: Solomon foresaw that trouble would come and that sin would move it; affliction drives to God even those who had said 'Depart.'

themes: the road home built in beforehand
the temple a refuge for the beaten

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'for thou pointest them to the good way,' a construction found only here, and judges the older reading at 1 Kings 8:36 probably right, a single letter altered.
Henry gathers this with vv.25, 38, 39 as the prayer that God would return in mercy to the penitent — which we may pray for, building on his declared readiness to accept penitents.

themes: forgive, then teach, then send rain
the good way pointed by God

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'if his enemies besiege him' (Kings 'enemy'), finds 'in the cities of their land' difficult (literally 'in the land of his gates'), and inclines with Bertheau to 'in the land, at his gates.'
Henry sets the catalogue of calamities among Solomon's sober suppositions — no prosperity is proof against trouble, and each stroke is meant to drive the people to God.

themes: every stroke a summons to the sanctuary

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the relative 'when'/'if' as in the earlier cases, and notes 'his own sore and his own grief' reads like the chronicler's gloss on the older 'plague of his own heart.'

themes: from the many to the one
the temple opened to the solitary sufferer

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'the man' (distributive) and 'because thou knowest his heart,' making God's knowledge the ground of the just recompense; 'all' has dropped from the received text.
Henry seizes the parenthesis: God alone knows the hearts of men, all thoughts and affections naked before him — he knows the heart itself and 'all the beatings of it.'

themes: render to each according to his ways

schools.summary: Ellicott notes 'to walk in thy ways' is an explanatory remark added by the chronicler, fixing the aim of the plea — that mercy should breed a practical, obedient fear of God.

themes: forgiven that God may be feared
reverence and the tenure of the land

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the chronicler has run together 1 Kings 8:41-42, and restores the fuller original — the stranger drawn by God's 'great name, mighty hand, and stretched-out arm.'
Henry: strangers would come from afar, considering how worthless the gods of the nations were and what proofs the God of Israel had given of being Lord of the whole earth.

themes: the stranger from a far country

schools.summary: Ellicott corrects 'people' to 'the peoples' and reads the closing clause 'that they may fear thee'; the chronicler's opening 'and' agrees with the earlier cases.
Henry: God would bid the strangers welcome and answer their prayers — 'one law for the stranger and for one born in the land' (Lev. 24:22).

themes: that all peoples may know the name
the missionary reach of the house

schools.summary: Ellicott fixes 'toward this city' as literally 'the way of this city' — a bearing, the line along which prayer is aimed (cf. v.38) — the army praying with faces set toward Jerusalem.

themes: the war undertaken at God's sending
the battlefield an extension of the courts

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'maintain their cause' as 'accomplish their right' (Vulgate 'avenge them') — a plea for God to do Israel justice against the enemy.
Henry: God would own and plead his people's cause — 'if they be the Israel of God, their cause is the cause of God, and he would espouse it.'

themes: the people's quarrel made God's own
God staking his honour on his people

schools.summary: Ellicott weighs 'the land of their captivity' against the older 'land of their enemies,' suggesting 'their captivity' may be a corruption of 'their captors.'
Henry gathers this with v.39 as the prayer that God would return in mercy to the penitent who seeks him — resting on his declared readiness to accept them.

themes: praying toward land, city, and house
the three circles of the promise
the faced temple as pledge of welcome

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the plural 'supplications' probably not original, and marks the seam — 'forgive thy people' is the first clause of 1 Kings 8:50, after which Kings and Chronicles run wholly apart.
Henry reads it with v.35 as the plea that God would plead his people's cause, and with the earlier pleas as the plea for returning mercy — vindication and pardon joined.

themes: the climax of the seven cases
the seam where Kings and Chronicles part
sin the root, pardon the sufficient mercy

schools.summary: Ellicott links 'let thine eyes be open' to v.20 and forward to the divine answer at 2 Chron. 7:15; 'attent' (qashshuboth) is a rare word found elsewhere only in Ps. 130:2.

themes: a prayer about to be answered in its own words
